steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank fills an opening phrase before the main clause "it has since been translated into other languages." An introductory phrase cannot contain its own finite verb — it must use a non-finite form (like a past participle) to modify the subject of the main clause.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — is written: A present-tense finite verb. This creates an ungrammatical introductory phrase with a finite verb but no explicit subject. ✗
  • Choice B — had been written: A past perfect finite verb. Same problem as A: it creates an ungrammatical introductory phrase with a finite verb but no explicit subject. ✗
  • Choice C — written: A past participle used as a modifier. "Originally written in ancient Greek" is a participial phrase that correctly introduces the main clause — no finite verb, no structural problem. ✓
  • Choice D — was written: A simple past finite verb. Again, this creates an ungrammatical introductory phrase with a finite verb but no explicit subject. ✗
That is the correct answer.
